title: Rationalising Choice with Multi-self Models
abstract: Journal Article Rationalising Choice with Multi‐Self Models Get access Attila Ambrus, Attila Ambrus Duke University Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar Kareen Rozen Kareen Rozen Yale University Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar The Economic Journal, Volume 125, Issue 585, 1 June 2015, Pages 1136–1156, https://doi.org/10.1111/ecoj.12103 Published: 14 March 2014 Article history Received: 15 March 2013 Accepted: 04 September 2013 Published: 14 March 2014
summary_en: People sometimes make choices that look inconsistent across decisions — preferring A over B in one setting and B over A in another. Classical theory treats this as irrational. This paper offers a richer framework: the individual can be represented as a consistent aggregator of multiple "selves" with different preferences. The authors characterise which observed choice patterns are rationalisable by multi-self models and which are not. A technical contribution that gives behavioural economics a cleaner foundation for thinking about internal conflict and inconsistency.
policy_relevance: Foundational for behavioural consumer-protection analysis. Indirectly relevant to Hungarian retail-finance regulation and default design: if consumer inconsistency comes from multi-self conflict, then policies that commit consumers to their "long-run self" (automatic enrolment in savings, pre-commitment devices) can be welfare-improving. Caveat: the paper is theoretical; concrete Hungarian policy implications depend on empirical identification of which domain-specific multi-self conflicts matter most.
